story.title: Seasons and Sundays
story.description: Maya is ten years old and endlessly curious. On a rainy Saturday she asks her dad every question she can think of about the weather, and on Sunday morning the two of them make pancakes together — discovering that some of the best questions come from the kitchen.
story.author: ReadWell

vocabulary.definition: a description of what the weather will probably be like in the near future
vocabulary.exampleSentence: Maya asked her dad to check the weather forecast on his phone.

vocabulary.definition: very light rain that falls in fine, tiny drops
vocabulary.exampleSentence: Small drops of drizzle ran slowly down the window glass.

vocabulary.definition: the loud rumbling or crashing sound in the sky during a storm
vocabulary.exampleSentence: Maya was not frightened of thunder, but she always covered her ears.

vocabulary.definition: a thin white layer of ice crystals that forms on surfaces when the temperature falls below zero
vocabulary.exampleSentence: Maya had seen photographs of the garden covered in frost and wanted to see it herself.

vocabulary.definition: the line in the far distance where the sky appears to meet the land or sea
vocabulary.exampleSentence: Maya could see the horizon clearly — a thin line between the pale sky and the dark fields.

grammarFocus.explanation: In English, we form questions in different ways depending on the type. For Yes/No questions with most verbs, we use Do/Does (present) or Did (past) before the subject. For Wh- questions, we put the question word (What, When, Where, Why, How) before Do/Does/Did + subject. For subject questions, we use Who or What directly before the verb, without Do/Did.
examples: Does it always rain in October? (Yes/No question — does + subject + verb)
What is the weather forecast for today? (Wh- question — what + is + subject)
When did it last snow here? (Wh- question — when + did + subject + verb)
Who decides the weather forecast? (Subject question — who + verb, no Do/Did)
Do they always get the forecast right? (Yes/No question — do + subject + verb)
formGuide: Put the question word first when you need extra information, like what, where, when, why, or how.
Use do or does for present simple questions unless the question word is the subject.
Keep the verb be before the subject in questions with am, is, or are.
usageNotes: Use yes-no questions when you only need confirmation.
Use wh- questions when you need a specific detail or reason.
grammarFocus.contrastWith: Do not keep statement word order in a question. 'You like rainy days' is a statement, but 'Do you like rainy days?' is the question form.
commonMistakes: Leaving out do or does in a present simple question.
Using do or does after the verb be.
Keeping the subject before the auxiliary in a wh-question.

content.text: Maya woke up to a grey sky. She went downstairs and found her dad drinking tea by the window. Small drops of drizzle ran slowly down the glass. "Does it always rain in October?" Maya asked, sitting at the table. "Not always," her dad said. "But it does rain a lot in autumn." Maya looked out at the garden. The leaves on the trees were yellow and red, and one by one they fell softly to the ground.

"What is the weather forecast for today?" she asked. Her dad picked up his phone and checked the weather app. "More cloud in the morning," he said, "but it might clear up by the afternoon." Maya tilted her head. "Who decides the forecast?" she asked. "Do they always get it right?" Her dad laughed. "They use computers and a lot of data," he said. "But no — they don't always get it right." Maya nodded and made a note in the small notebook she kept on the kitchen table.

"When did it last snow here?" Maya asked. She had seen photographs of the garden covered in frost and wanted to see it herself. "It snowed last January," her dad said. "Did you forget? You wore three jumpers and made a tiny snowman." Maya laughed. She did remember — the snowman had fallen over in twenty minutes. "Does thunder happen in winter too?" she asked. "Sometimes," her dad said. "You can get a thunderstorm in any season. Winter ones are less common, though."

Later, the clouds began to part. Maya pressed her face against the glass. "Why does the sky look orange near the horizon?" she asked. Her dad came to look. "That happens when the sun is low," he said. "The light travels a longer distance through the air and the colours change." Maya thought about this for a moment. "What is the warmest month of the year?" she asked. Her dad folded his arms and looked at her with a smile. "Maya," he said gently, "do you ever stop asking questions?" Maya grinned. "No," she said. "I don't."

comprehension.question: What is the weather like when Maya looks out of the window in the morning?
options: Sunny with a few clouds
Grey and drizzly
Cold with frost on the ground
Stormy with thunder and lightning
comprehension.explanation: The text says 'Maya woke up to a grey sky' and 'small drops of drizzle ran slowly down the glass.'

comprehension.question: What does Maya's dad say about weather forecasters?
options: They always get the forecast right
They use computers and data but do not always get it right
They only check the weather once a day
They cannot predict rain more than one day in advance
comprehension.correctAnswer: They use computers and data but do not always get it right
comprehension.explanation: Maya's dad says: 'They use computers and a lot of data. But no — they don't always get it right.'

comprehension.question: What happened to Maya's snowman last January?
options: It melted in the sun by midday
It was knocked over by the wind
It fell over in twenty minutes
It was still standing the next morning
comprehension.correctAnswer: It fell over in twenty minutes
comprehension.explanation: The text says 'the snowman had fallen over in twenty minutes.'

comprehension.question: Why does the sky look orange near the horizon?
options: Because clouds reflect sunlight in the evening
Because the air is dirtier near the ground
Because the light travels a longer distance through the air when the sun is low
Because the temperature drops quickly after dark
comprehension.correctAnswer: Because the light travels a longer distance through the air when the sun is low
comprehension.explanation: Maya's dad explains: 'That happens when the sun is low. The light travels a longer distance through the air and the colours change.'

comprehension.question: What does Maya writing things in her notebook suggest about her character?
options: She is worried she will forget what her dad tells her
She is doing homework about the weather for school
She is curious and likes to record what she learns
She wants to send her notes to a weather forecaster
comprehension.correctAnswer: She is curious and likes to record what she learns
comprehension.explanation: Maya asks many questions and writes them down — this shows she is a curious person who enjoys learning and keeping a record of what she discovers.

grammarPractice.prompt: Choose the correct question about today's forecast.
options: What the forecast says for today?
What does the forecast say for today?
What does say the forecast for today?
What is the forecast say for today?
grammarPractice.correctAnswer: What does the forecast say for today?
grammarPractice.explanation: Use does + subject + base verb for a present simple wh-question.

grammarPractice.prompt: Complete the question: ___ are you carrying an umbrella today?
Correct the question: You like rainy days?
grammarPractice.explanation: Present simple yes-no questions need do or does.

grammarPractice.prompt: Rewrite as a question: He is leaving now. Ask about the time.
grammarPractice.explanation: Use when to ask about time with the verb be before the subject.

grammarPractice.prompt: Rewrite as a question: Maya wants to know the warmest month of the year.
grammarPractice.correctAnswer: What is the warmest month of the year?
grammarPractice.explanation: Use what for the missing information and keep is before the subject phrase.

vocabulary.definition: a fine white powder made from ground wheat, used to make bread, cakes, and pastries
vocabulary.exampleSentence: Maya's dad poured the flour carefully into the bowl and smoothed it with a spoon.

vocabulary.definition: any item of food that is combined with others to make a dish or recipe
vocabulary.exampleSentence: Maya read each ingredient from the recipe card one by one.

vocabulary.definition: a combination of different foods stirred or blended together
vocabulary.exampleSentence: Maya stirred the eggs, flour, and milk until the mixture was smooth and pale yellow.

vocabulary.definition: a single piece of bread baked in one block shape, usually sliced for toast or sandwiches
vocabulary.exampleSentence: There was a fresh loaf of bread on the counter next to the toaster.

vocabulary.definition: the amount of a particular food that is served to one person
vocabulary.exampleSentence: Her dad put a generous portion of pancakes on Maya's plate.

grammarFocus.explanation: Countable nouns are things we can count one by one. They have singular and plural forms, and we can use a/an or numbers with them. Uncountable nouns are things we usually measure or talk about as a whole, so we do not say 'a flour' or 'two milks'. With countable nouns we use many and a few. With uncountable nouns we use much and a little. We can use some, any, and a lot of with both types.
examples: There are three eggs in the fridge. (countable — can be counted, plural form)
There was not much flour left in the bag. (uncountable — use much, not many)
Can I have a little honey, please? (uncountable — a little, not a few)
How many strawberries do you want? (countable — use many)
There is a lot of milk in the fridge. (a lot of — works with both types)
formGuide: Countable nouns can be singular or plural and can use numbers.
Uncountable nouns do not usually use a plural form and are measured with words like much, a little, or some.
Use many or a few with countable nouns and much or a little with uncountable nouns.
usageNotes: Choose the noun type first, then choose the matching quantifier.
Use some with both countable plurals and uncountable nouns in positive sentences.
grammarFocus.contrastWith: Do not treat uncountable nouns like countable ones. You can say 'some milk' or 'a bottle of milk', but not 'three milks' in this context.
commonMistakes: Using many with an uncountable noun.
Using a plural verb with an uncountable noun when the sentence is singular.
Forgetting that some nouns need a container phrase to become countable.

content.text: On Sunday morning, Maya's dad announced that he was going to make pancakes. "Can I help?" Maya asked. "Of course," he said. He pulled a recipe card from the kitchen drawer. "We need flour, milk, and eggs. Do we have any eggs?" Maya checked the fridge. "There are three," she said. "Is that enough?" Her dad studied the card. "We need four. I will also use some flour from that bag." He looked at the flour bag — it was almost empty. "There is not much flour left," Maya said.

Her dad read out the list of ingredients. "Two hundred grams of flour, two eggs, and some milk." He looked at Maya. "How much milk is in the fridge?" Maya checked. "A lot," she said. "Almost a full bottle." He cracked two eggs into a bowl and added the flour. "How many ingredients are in the recipe?" Maya asked, following along. "Just three main ones," he said. "But you can add a little sugar or a few drops of vanilla if you like." Maya stirred the mixture slowly while her dad poured in the milk.

While the batter rested, her dad put some bread in the toaster. There was a fresh loaf on the counter. He cut two thick slices. "Would you like some honey?" he asked, taking a small jar from the shelf. "Yes please," said Maya. Her dad tilted the jar. "There is not much honey left," he said. "Just a little — enough for one small portion each." Maya nodded. She liked having exactly the right portion — not too much, not too little.

The pancakes were ready in a few minutes. Her dad put two portions on Maya's plate and added some strawberries from a bowl on the table. "How many strawberries do you want?" he asked. "A few," she said. "Not too many." The pancakes were warm and soft. "This is the best breakfast," Maya announced. "Can we have pancakes every Sunday?" Her dad poured himself some tea and sat down. "We would need more eggs for that," he said, "and more flour." Maya picked up her fork. "But there is a lot of milk," she said. Her dad laughed.

comprehension.question: Why does Maya's dad say they need to buy more eggs?
options: The recipe needs four eggs but there are only three in the fridge
The eggs in the fridge are too old to use
He wants to make scrambled eggs as well as pancakes
Maya accidentally dropped one on the floor
comprehension.correctAnswer: The recipe needs four eggs but there are only three in the fridge
comprehension.explanation: Maya finds three eggs in the fridge, but her dad says the recipe needs four.

comprehension.question: How much milk does Maya say is in the fridge?
options: Not much, just a little
None — they need to buy some
A lot, almost a full bottle
Exactly enough for the recipe
comprehension.correctAnswer: A lot, almost a full bottle
comprehension.explanation: Maya checks the fridge and says: 'A lot. Almost a full bottle.'

comprehension.question: Why can Maya and her dad only have a small amount of honey?
options: Because too much honey is not healthy
Because Maya's dad does not like honey very much
Because there is not much honey left in the jar
Because the recipe says to use only a small amount
comprehension.correctAnswer: Because there is not much honey left in the jar
comprehension.explanation: Maya's dad tilts the jar and says: 'There is not much honey left. Just a little — enough for one small portion each.'

comprehension.question: How many portions of pancakes does Maya's dad put on her plate?
options: One
Two
Three
Four
comprehension.explanation: The text says: 'Her dad put two portions on Maya's plate.'

comprehension.question: At the end of the story, why does Maya say 'But there is a lot of milk'?
options: She wants to remind her dad to check the fridge before shopping
She is asking if they should use the milk before it goes bad
She is playfully joining in with the list of what they have and do not have
She wants to add more milk to her pancakes
comprehension.correctAnswer: She is playfully joining in with the list of what they have and do not have
comprehension.explanation: When her dad lists what they would need more of (eggs, flour), Maya cheerfully adds what they do have plenty of — milk. It is a playful, humorous moment between them.

options: There are many butter left in the dish.
There is much butter left in the dish.
There is a butter left in the dish.
There are a few butter left in the dish.
grammarPractice.correctAnswer: There is much butter left in the dish.
grammarPractice.explanation: Butter is uncountable, so use is and much.

grammarPractice.prompt: Complete the sentence: How ___ milk do we need for the pancakes?
grammarPractice.explanation: Use much with uncountable nouns like milk.

grammarPractice.prompt: Correct the sentence: There are too many sugar in the bowl.
grammarPractice.correctAnswer: There is too much sugar in the bowl.
grammarPractice.explanation: Sugar is uncountable, so use is and much.

grammarPractice.prompt: Choose the sentence with countable nouns used correctly.
options: We bought much apples and some cheese.
We bought a few apples and some cheese.
We bought many cheese and an apple.
We bought a little apples and some cheese.
grammarPractice.correctAnswer: We bought a few apples and some cheese.
grammarPractice.explanation: Use a few with plural countable nouns like apples.

grammarPractice.prompt: Rewrite using much: There isn't a lot of flour left in the bag.
grammarPractice.correctAnswer: There isn't much flour left in the bag.
grammarPractice.explanation: Flour is uncountable, so much is the natural quantifier here.
